perturbation.original_text: The Investor agrees to make, and the applicable Borrower(s) agree(s) to accept, Required Capital Contributions in respect of the applicable 12 Month Period (or shorter period prior to July 3, 2006, as applicable) in an amount sufficient to cause (via an addition to Adjusted EBITDAR in the amount of such Required Capital Contributions) the Borrowers to comply with Section 2 of Schedule 10.28 to the Financing Agreement as of the last day of each Fiscal Quarter.
perturbation.changed_text: The Investor will endeavor to make, and the applicable Borrower(s) will attempt to accept, Required Capital Contributions in respect of the applicable 12 Month Period (or shorter period prior to July 3, 2006, as applicable) in an amount that could potentially assist the Borrowers in generally aligning with Section 2 of Schedule 10.28 to the Financing Agreement as of the last day of each Fiscal Quarter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to make' to 'will endeavor to make' introduces ambiguity, making the capital contribution discretionary. Similarly, 'agree(s) to accept' is changed to 'will attempt to accept', which weakens the borrower's obligation to accept the contribution. The phrase 'in an amount sufficient to cause...the Borrowers to comply' is replaced with 'in an amount that could potentially assist the Borrowers in generally aligning with', significantly weakening the obligation to comply with the financial requirements, rendering the required capital contributions practically unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 336.3-603 Discharge of Obligation by Payment
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 336.3-603 addresses the discharge of obligations by payment. By weakening the investor's commitment to make capital contributions and the borrower's commitment to accept them, the modification contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in the enforcement of contracts. The original text clearly defined the Investor's obligation to make sufficient capital contributions and the Borrower's obligation to accept them, ensuring compliance with a specific section of the Financing Agreement. Making these actions discretionary creates uncertainty and could allow the Investor or Borrower to avoid their responsibilities, hindering the discharge of obligations as agreed upon in the Maintenance Agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.3-603 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.3-603 336.3-602 336.3-604 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.3-603 Version List 336.3-603 MS 1990 [Repealed, 1992 c 565 s 114 ] 336.3-603 TENDER OF PAYMENT. (a) If tender of payment of an obligation to pay an instrument is made to a person entitled to enforce the instrument, the effect of tender is governed by principles of law applicable to tender of payment under a simple contract. (b) If tender of payment of an obligation to pay an instrument is made to a person entitled to enforce the instrument and the tender is refused, there is discharge, to the extent of the amount of the tender, of the obligation of an endorser or accommodation party having a right of recourse with respect to the obligation to which the tender relates. (c) If tender of payment of an amount due on an instrument is made to a person entitled to enforce the instrument, the obligation of the obligor to pay interest after the due date on the amount tendered is discharged. If presentment is required with respect to an instrument and the obligor is able and ready to pay on the due date at every place of payment stated in the instrument, the obligor is deemed to have made tender of payment on the due date to the person entitled to enforce the instrument. History: 1992 c 565 s 68 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandated the Investor to 'agree to make' capital contributions and the Borrower to 'agree to accept' them to comply with specific financial requirements. The changed text weakens these obligations by using phrases like 'will endeavor to make' and 'attempt to accept,' and replacing 'sufficient to cause...the Borrowers to comply' with 'that could potentially assist the Borrowers in generally aligning with.' This introduces ambiguity and makes compliance practically unenforceable, directly contradicting the original intent. While the snippet focuses on 'tender of payment' which isn't directly about the obligation to pay, the law explanation connects this to the implied duty of good faith, arguing that weakening commitments undermines contract enforcement. The ambiguity introduced means there's no guaranteed or enforceable way to ensure that the Borrowers comply with section 2 of Schedule 10.28 which represents a significant departure from the original legally enforceable terms.

perturbation.original_text: Specific Performance and Liquidated Damages. The Investor acknowledges and agrees that if a Maintenance Event of Default (as defined in Section 10 below) has occurred and is continuing hereunder, the Agent, for itself and on behalf of the Lenders, shall have the non-exclusive right to obtain specific performance of the obligation of the Investor to make the Required Capital Contributions.
perturbation.changed_text: Specific Performance and Liquidated Damages. The Investor acknowledges that if a Maintenance Event of Default (as defined in Section 10 below) has occurred, the Agent, for itself and on behalf of the Lenders, may consider seeking specific performance of the obligation of the Investor to make the Required Capital Contributions, subject to their discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides the Agent with the 'non-exclusive right to obtain specific performance' of the Investor's obligation. The modified text changes this to 'may consider seeking specific performance...subject to their discretion'. This makes the remedy of specific performance discretionary rather than a defined right, significantly weakening the Agent's ability to enforce the contract and creating ambiguity around available remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-716 Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-716 grants a buyer the right to specific performance under certain circumstances. While this statute primarily deals with the sale of goods, the underlying principle of specific performance as a remedy for breach of contract is relevant. By making specific performance discretionary, the modified text contradicts the principle that specific performance should be available as a remedy when monetary damages are inadequate. The original language clearly stated the Agent's right to seek specific performance, aligning with the intent of contract law to provide effective remedies for breach. The modified language introduces uncertainty and allows the Agent to potentially avoid seeking specific performance even when it may be the most appropriate remedy, undermining the enforceability of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.2-716 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.2-716 336.2-715 336.2-717 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.2-716 Version List Recent History 2000 336.2-716 Amended 2000 c 399 art 2 s 7 336.2-716 BUYER'S RIGHT TO SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OR REPLEVIN. (1) Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. (2) The decree for specific performance may include such terms and conditions as to payment of the price, damages, or other relief as the court may deem just. (3) The buyer has a right of replevin for goods identified to the contract if after reasonable effort the buyer is unable to effect cover for such goods or the circumstances reasonably indicate that such effort will be unavailing or if the goods have been shipped under reservation and satisfaction of the security interest in them has been made or tendered. In the case of goods bought for personal, family, or household purposes, the buyer's right of replevin vests upon acquisition of a special property, even if the seller had not then repudiated or failed to deliver. History: 1965 c 811 s 336 .2-716; 1986 c 444 ; 2000 c 399 art 2 s 7 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall have the non-exclusive right to obtain specific performance' to 'may consider seeking specific performance...subject to their discretion' introduces ambiguity, weakening the enforceability of the Investor's obligation. Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-716 states, 'Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances.' While this pertains to the sale of goods, the underlying principle of specific performance as a remedy is relevant. The original contract language established a clear right, whereas the modified language makes this right discretionary, potentially undermining the ability to obtain specific performance even when appropriate. This aligns with the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition, where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce, and leaving legal obligations open to dispute.
